case_name: State Of H.P vs Krishan Lal
court: High Court of Himachal Pradesh, Shimla
facts: On 30.8.2006 at about 4:20 pm a vehicle bearing registration No. HP-01-3243 driven by Krishan Lal allegedly met with an accident at Thachi after going half a kilometre and fell into a gorge. Occupants included Mohan Lal, Yog Raj, Virender Sharma, Sanju/Anju and others; Yog Raj, Paras Ram and Besar Dutt died on the spot; others sustained injuries including a compound fracture and extradural hematoma. Police investigated, seized the vehicle and documents, obtained medical and post-mortem reports, and submitted a challan charging the accused under Sections 279, 337, 338 and 304-A IPC and Section 186 of the Motor Vehicles Act. At trial the accused admitted driving but denied rash/negligent driving, asserting the accident occurred due to sudden collapse of a retaining wall.
arguments_claimant: The State urged that the trial court misappreciated prosecution evidence and set unrealistic standards; multiple witnesses had stated the accused was driving at high speed causing the accident. The State submitted the investigating officer's site plan did not show retaining wall collapse and that the accused should have been vigilant in rain; hence the acquittal was perverse and should be set aside.
arguments_respondent: The accused contended that prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt and that the doctrine of res ipsa loquitur does not warrant conviction in criminal cases; 'high speed' alone does not prove rashness or negligence. The respondent relied on precedents (e.g. S.L. Goswami, Syad Akbar) and maintained the accident plausibly occurred due to collapse of a retaining wall, a version accepted by several prosecution witnesses on cross-examination.
ratio_decidendi: "It is the cardinal principle of criminal jurisprudence that there is a presumption of innocence in favour of the accused unless proven guilty. The presumption of innocence gets concretised when the case ends in acquittal. ... If two views are possible from the evidence on record, the appellate court must be extremely slow in interfering with the appeal against acquittal." "The maxim res ipsa loquitur... only as a convenient ratiocinative aid in the assessment of evidence, in drawing permissive inferences under Section 114 of the Evidence Act... Such simplified and pragmatic application of the notion of res ipsa loquitur... is subject to all the principles...: Firstly, all the circumstances... must be firmly established. Secondly, those circumstances must be of a determinative tendency pointing unerringly towards the guilt of the accused. Thirdly, the circumstances shown make a chain so complete that they cannot reasonably raise any other hypothesis save that of the accused's guilt." Applying these principles, the Court held the trial court's acceptance of the retaining-wall collapse explanation was a "possible view" based on record and therefore could not be disturbed.
final_order: The appeal is dismissed and the judgment of acquittal passed by the Trial Court is upheld; the respondent/accused shall be released immediately if not required in any other case and the trial court record is returned.
summary: This is a criminal appeal by the State against the acquittal of Krishan Lal for an accident on 30.8.2006 in which three persons died and others were injured. The High Court reviewed law on appellate interference and the doctrine of res ipsa loquitur, and found the trial court's acceptance of a plausible version (collapse of a retaining wall) to be a permissible view on evidence. Because two views were possible and the acquittal reinforced the presumption of innocence, the High Court refused to disturb the acquittal and dismissed the appeal. No monetary compensation issues arise in this judgment.
embedding_summary: State of H.P. v. Krishan Lal, High Court of Himachal Pradesh, Cr. Appeal No. 189 of 2011, Neutral Citation No. (2024:HHC:8595), Decision 13.9.2024. Motor-vehicle accident dated 30.8.2006 at Thachi involving vehicle registration HP-01-3243; three fatalities (Paras Ram, Yog Raj, Besar Dutt) and multiple injuries (Virender, Sanju/Anju etc.). Charges under Sections 279, 337, 338, 304-A IPC and Section 186 Motor Vehicles Act; investigation, site plan and mechanical report produced. Key legal issues: scope of appellate interference in appeals against acquittal (two-views doctrine), applicability and limits of res ipsa loquitur in criminal trials as a permissive inference under Section 114 Evidence Act, burden-shifting only where attendant circumstances are determinative, and evidentiary value of witnesses' statements about 'high speed'. Court applied precedents (Mallappa, Sanjeev, Noor Aga, Syad Akbar, Ravi Kapur, Thakur Singh etc.), held trial court's view was a plausible view on record and dismissed the State's appeal; acquittal maintained and accused ordered released. No compensation awarded.
legal_principles: Appellate restraint in appeals against acquittal: interference permitted only if trial court's view is illegal or perverse; where 'two views' are possible, the view favoring the accused should prevail
Presumption of innocence is reinforced by an acquittal and requires a higher threshold in appeal to overturn

Res ipsa loquitur may be used in criminal trials only as a permissive inference under Section 114 Evidence Act, not as an automatic reverse burden, and only when attendant circumstances are firmly established
Reverse burden may shift to the person in control of the vehicle to explain the accident, but only where the circumstances point unerringly to negligence and exclude other hypotheses
Bare assertions of 'high speed' by witnesses are insufficient to prove rashness/negligence; witnesses cannot give opinion evidence on negligence without supporting facts

bm25_text: State Of H.P vs Krishan Lal High Court of Himachal Pradesh, Shimla 2024 On 30.8.2006 at about 4:20 pm a vehicle bearing registration No. HP-01-3243 driven by Krishan Lal allegedly met with an accident at Thachi after going half a kilometre and fell into a gorge. Occupants included Mohan Lal, Yog Raj, Virender Sharma, Sanju/Anju and others; Yog Raj, Paras Ram and Besar Dutt died on the spot; others sustained injuries including a compound fracture and extradural hematoma. Police investigated, seized the vehicle and documents, obtained medical and post-mortem reports, and submitted a challan charging the accused under Sections 279, 337, 338 and 304-A IPC and Section 186 of the Motor Vehicles Act. At trial the accused admitted driving but denied rash/negligent driving, asserting the accident occurred due to sudden collapse of a retaining wall. The State urged that the trial court misappreciated prosecution evidence and set unrealistic standards; multiple witnesses had stated the accused was driving at high speed causing the accident. The State submitted the investigating officer's site plan did not show retaining wall collapse and that the accused should have been vigilant in rain; hence the acquittal was perverse and should be set aside. The accused contended that prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt and that the doctrine of res ipsa loquitur does not warrant conviction in criminal cases; 'high speed' alone does not prove rashness or negligence. The respondent relied on precedents (e.g. S.L. Goswami, Syad Akbar) and maintained the accident plausibly occurred due to collapse of a retaining wall, a version accepted by several prosecution witnesses on cross-examination. "It is the cardinal principle of criminal jurisprudence that there is a presumption of innocence in favour of the accused unless proven guilty. The presumption of innocence gets concretised when the case ends in acquittal. ... If two views are possible from the evidence on record, the appellate court must be extremely slow in interfering with the appeal against acquittal." "The maxim res ipsa loquitur... only as a convenient ratiocinative aid in the assessment of evidence, in drawing permissive inferences under Section 114 of the Evidence Act... Such simplified and pragmatic application of the notion of res ipsa loquitur... is subject to all the principles...: Firstly, all the circumstances... must be firmly established. Secondly, those circumstances must be of a determinative tendency pointing unerringly towards the guilt of the accused. Thirdly, the circumstances shown make a chain so complete that they cannot reasonably raise any other hypothesis save that of the accused's guilt." Applying these principles, the Court held the trial court's acceptance of the retaining-wall collapse explanation was a "possible view" based on record and therefore could not be disturbed. The appeal is dismissed and the judgment of acquittal passed by the Trial Court is upheld; the respondent/accused shall be released immediately if not required in any other case and the trial court record is returned. This is a criminal appeal by the State against the acquittal of Krishan Lal for an accident on 30.8.2006 in which three persons died and others were injured. The High Court reviewed law on appellate interference and the doctrine of res ipsa loquitur, and found the trial court's acceptance of a plausible version (collapse of a retaining wall) to be a permissible view on evidence. Because two views were possible and the acquittal reinforced the presumption of innocence, the High Court refused to disturb the acquittal and dismissed the appeal. No monetary compensation issues arise in this judgment. State of H.P. v. Krishan Lal, High Court of Himachal Pradesh, Cr.
